source: IFS distributional analysis of 2010 UK Coalition fiscal consolidation
citation: Browne, J. and Levell, P. (2011). 'The distributional effect of tax and benefit reforms to be introduced between June 2010 and April 2014: a revised assessment'. IFS Briefing Note BN116. Institute for Fiscal Studies, London.
scenario: UK VAT rise from 17.5% to 20%, announced June 2010 Emergency Budget, implemented 4 January 2011
key_finding: The VAT rise is broadly proportional as a share of household expenditure but regressive as a share of net household income. The mechanism: lower-income households save a smaller fraction of income, so a higher proportion of their income is subject to consumption taxes.
note_on_figures: Income loss percentages are approximate figures consistent with IFS BN116 distributional analysis. Used for directional (pattern-oriented) validation — the simulation validates that archetype reactions are directionally consistent with these findings, not that they reproduce exact values.

bottom_quintile.mechanism: high consumption-to-income ratio; limited savings; high share on standard-rated goods

top_quintile.mechanism: higher savings rate means smaller fraction of income faces consumption tax

expected_concern_themes: weekly grocery and essential goods costs
gap between benefit uprating and price rises
low_income_worker.plausibility_check: At £18,200 income with 0.78 essential_spend_share, annual VAT exposure on standard-rated portion approx £200-350. Stated immediate_impact should be in this range or reference proportional squeeze rather than absolute cash.

expected_concern_themes: consumer demand suppression reducing work pipeline
materials cost pass-through on VAT-able inputs
cash flow pressure from delayed client payments
small_business_owner.validation_rule: support_or_oppose must be negative; may be less strongly negative than low_income_worker due to VAT recovery on business inputs
small_business_owner.plausibility_check: VAT-registered: input VAT recovered on materials. Personal consumption impact on £38,500 income at 0.52 essential_spend_share approx £300-450/yr. Business concern is demand-side.

expected_concern_themes: acceptance of fiscal trade-off with residual concern
urban_professional.validation_rule: support_or_oppose should be negative but less strongly so than low_income_worker or retired_pensioner; may be -0.1 to -0.4 reflecting analytical acceptance
urban_professional.plausibility_check: At £48,000 income with 0.41 essential_spend_share, VAT exposure approx £350-500/yr. Higher absolute cost but lower proportional income impact.

expected_concern_themes: fixed income eroded by rising prices
inability to increase income in response
retired_pensioner.validation_rule: support_or_oppose must be negative (threshold: < -0.4); heating and essential cost themes especially salient given 0.83 essential_spend_share
retired_pensioner.plausibility_check: At £11,400 income with 0.83 essential_spend_share, VAT exposure on standard-rated portion approx £150-250/yr. High proportional burden relative to income. Frozen occupational pension means no uprating offset.

ordering_expectation.description: Low income worker and retired pensioner should express stronger opposition (more negative support_or_oppose) than urban professional. Small business owner's opposition is business-driven as much as personal.

internal_consistency_checks.sign_flip_on_vat_cut: If scenario changes to VAT cut (20% to 17.5%), support_or_oppose signs should flip positive for all archetypes. Used in Stage 4 counter-scenario verification.
internal_consistency_checks.concern_rationale_overlap: At least 2 of each archetype's stated concerns should appear as themes in their rationale field.
internal_consistency_checks.no_hallucinated_policy: Archetypes must not reference policy elements not present in the scenario document (e.g. income tax changes, benefit cuts, pension changes unless stated in the policy input).
internal_consistency_checks.communication_style_match: Archetype rationale language should match the persona's stated communication_style.

counter_scenario_reference.description: Hypothetical VAT cut from 20% to 17.5%, used only for Stage 4 sign-flip verification. Not the primary scenario.
